title: LLM Evaluation Fundamentals
description: Design rigorous evaluation frameworks for LLMs — benchmarks, human evaluation protocols, automatic metrics, and the evaluation pyramid from unit tests to production monitoring.
learningObjectives: Distinguish task-specific metrics from general capability benchmarks
Design human evaluation rubrics with inter-annotator agreement scoring
Implement automatic evaluation using BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore, and G-Eval
Build an evaluation pyramid from unit tests to production monitoring
Detect evaluation gaming and Goodhart's Law failures in LLM systems

sections.title: Why Rigorous Evaluation is the Foundation of Production LLMs
sections.content: Shipping an LLM feature without an evaluation framework is flying blind — you don't know if version 2 is better or worse than version 1, whether a prompt change helped or hurt, or whether cost cuts degraded quality. Evaluation is what turns subjective 'it seems better' into measurable, defensible decisions. It's also the mechanism that catches regressions before users do. Every production LLM system needs: offline evaluation before deployment, online monitoring after deployment, and human evaluation as the ground truth calibrator.

sections.title: The LLM Evaluation Landscape
sections.content:
## Evaluation Pyramid
```
            ┌─────────────────────┐
            │   Production Metrics │  ← user satisfaction, retention
         ┌──┴─────────────────────┴──┐
         │    Online A/B Tests       │  ← live traffic comparison
      ┌──┴───────────────────────────┴──┐
      │       Human Evaluation          │  ← ground truth labelling
   ┌──┴─────────────────────────────────┴──┐
   │      Automatic Evaluation (LLM-judge)  │  ← scalable, fast
┌──┴────────────────────────────────────────┴──┐
│     Unit Tests / Golden Dataset               │  ← cheap, deterministic
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Metric Categories

| Category | Metrics | Use Case |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **Lexical overlap** | BLEU, ROUGE-L | Translation, summarisation |
| **Semantic similarity** | BERTScore, cosine sim | Paraphrase detection |
| **LLM-as-judge** | G-Eval, pairwise | Open-ended generation |
| **Task-specific** | F1, accuracy, EM | Classification, QA |
| **Human** | Likert scale, preference | Ground truth calibration |

## BLEU vs ROUGE
- **BLEU**: precision-based — what fraction of the generated n-grams appear in the reference?
- **ROUGE-L**: recall-based — what fraction of the reference n-grams appear in the generation?
- Both fail on semantic equivalence: 'car' vs 'automobile' scores 0 despite being correct

## BERTScore
Computes token-level similarity between hypothesis and reference using contextual embeddings:
```
Precision = avg max cosine_sim(generated_token, reference_tokens)
Recall    = avg max cosine_sim(reference_token, generated_tokens)
F1        = harmonic mean of precision and recall
```
Much better than BLEU for semantic capture.

## G-Eval (LLM-based)
Use an LLM to score output on multiple dimensions:
```
Score each on 1-5:
- Coherence: is the response logically structured?
- Relevance: does it address the question?
- Fluency: is the language natural?
- Faithfulness: are facts grounded in the provided context?
```

## Inter-Annotator Agreement
Human evaluations are only valid when annotators agree. Measure with:
- **Cohen's Kappa**: accounts for chance agreement; κ > 0.6 = substantial agreement
- **Krippendorff's Alpha**: works for ordinal scales and more than 2 annotators

## Goodhart's Law in LLM Evaluation
'When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.' Examples:
- Optimising BLEU score leads to short, high-precision outputs that miss key information
- Optimising for a single LLM judge leads to outputs that exploit the judge's biases
- Always use multiple metrics; never optimise for just one

sections.title: Evaluation Suite — Automatic Metrics + LLM Judge
Evaluation Framework Pitfalls
sections.content:
**1. Single-metric optimisation (Goodhart's Law)** — Optimising BLEU alone produces short, high-precision outputs that miss key information. Always use a metric ensemble; weight metrics according to what matters for the specific task.

**2. Reference answer quality as the ceiling** — If reference answers are written by non-experts, BLEU/ROUGE scores are meaningless. Invest in high-quality references; a bad reference set makes all derived metrics unreliable.

**3. LLM judge self-preference bias** — GPT-4 used as a judge tends to prefer GPT-4-generated outputs. Use a judge model different from the model being evaluated, or use multiple judges and average.

**4. Not measuring inter-annotator agreement** — Human evaluation with low agreement (κ < 0.4) is noise, not signal. Measure Cohen's Kappa before relying on human labels. If agreement is low, revise the rubric.

**5. Evaluating on the training distribution** — If your golden dataset overlaps with the model's fine-tuning data, scores are inflated. Hold out a truly unseen test set; never train and evaluate on the same data.

**6. Ignoring evaluation cost** — Running LLM-as-judge on every inference in production costs 2× the original inference. Gate expensive evaluation: run LLM judge on a sampled 5% of traffic; use fast automatic metrics on the rest.

**7. Not tracking evaluation over time** — A one-time evaluation score is a snapshot. Track scores as a time series — if they drift down as traffic patterns change, you detect quality degradation before users do.

sections.title: Interview Questions — LLM Evaluation
sections.content:
## Conceptual
**Q: Why is BLEU score insufficient for evaluating LLM-generated text?**
A: BLEU measures n-gram precision against a reference — it cannot detect semantic equivalence. 'automobile' and 'car' score 0 similarity despite being synonymous. It also penalises paraphrases that are actually better than the reference. For LLM evaluation, semantic metrics (BERTScore) and LLM-as-judge (G-Eval) capture quality that BLEU misses. BLEU remains useful as a cheap sanity check alongside better metrics.

## Code-Based
**Q: Your LLM judge consistently gives higher scores to longer answers. How do you detect and fix this?**
A: Detection: compute correlation between `len(generated_answer)` and `llm_overall_score` across your eval set — if Pearson correlation > 0.5, the judge has a length bias. Fix: (1) add explicit length-penalty instruction: 'do not prefer longer answers over concise correct ones'; (2) normalise scores against a length-stratified baseline; (3) switch to pairwise comparison (A vs B, which is better?) rather than absolute scoring — pairwise is more robust to length bias.

## Senior Scenario
**Q: You ship a prompt change that improves BLEU by 0.05 but LLM-judge relevance drops by 0.3. Which change do you accept?**
A: Reject the change. BLEU improvement (0.05 on a 0-1 scale) represents minor surface-level similarity gain. LLM-judge relevance drop (0.3 on a 0-1 scale, equivalent to 1.5 Likert points) is a substantial quality degradation — the answer is less relevant to the question. When metrics conflict, LLM-judge relevance is a stronger signal of user-perceived quality than lexical overlap. Run human evaluation on 50 samples to validate the LLM-judge finding before making the final decision.

## Wrong Answers to Avoid
- 'A higher BLEU score always means a better answer' — BLEU measures n-gram overlap, not quality or relevance
- 'LLM-as-judge is objective' — LLM judges have biases (verbosity, self-preference, position bias); calibrate them against human labels
- 'Evaluate once before launch and you are done' — production data distribution shifts over time; evaluation must be continuous

questions.question: What does ROUGE-L measure in text evaluation?
options: The precision of n-grams in the generated text against a reference
The F1 score based on the longest common subsequence between hypothesis and reference
The semantic similarity between hypothesis and reference using embeddings
The perplexity of the generated text under a language model
questions.explanation: ROUGE-L uses the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) to compute precision, recall, and F1. Unlike BLEU (n-gram precision), ROUGE-L captures recall — how much of the reference content appears in the hypothesis — and handles non-contiguous matches via LCS.

questions.question: Why should the LLM used as a judge be different from the model being evaluated?
options: Different models have different API rate limits
LLMs have self-preference bias — they tend to score outputs from the same model higher
Using the same model creates circular dependency in the API calls
The evaluation model requires more parameters than the generation model
questions.explanation: Research shows LLMs rate outputs from models with similar training as higher quality than outputs from other models. Using a different judge model (e.g., evaluating GPT-4o outputs with Claude, or vice versa) reduces this self-preference bias.

questions.question: Cohen's Kappa κ = 0.35 between two human annotators. What does this mean?
options: Annotators agree on 35% of cases — acceptable for subjective tasks
Agreement is only slightly better than chance — the rubric needs revision
35% of annotations should be discarded
One annotator is significantly more accurate than the other
questions.explanation: Cohen's Kappa accounts for chance agreement. κ = 0.35 is classified as 'fair' agreement — only slightly above chance. This means the annotation rubric is unclear or the task is too subjective. Labels from this pair cannot be reliably used as ground truth; revise the rubric and re-annotate.

questions.question: What is Goodhart's Law and how does it apply to LLM evaluation?
options: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure — optimising a single eval metric leads to gaming that metric at the expense of true quality
Larger models always score better on evaluation benchmarks regardless of task
Human evaluation is always more accurate than automatic evaluation
Evaluation costs are proportional to model size
questions.explanation: Goodhart's Law: when you optimise BLEU, you get outputs that maximise n-gram overlap but may be less informative. When you optimise a single LLM judge, outputs learn to exploit that judge's biases. Always use multiple diverse metrics and validate against human ground truth.

questions.question: You want to compare two model versions on a 1000-sample dataset. LLM-judge cost is $0.001 per sample. What is the most cost-effective evaluation strategy?
options: Run LLM judge on all 1000 samples for both versions
Run cheap metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) on all samples; run LLM judge only on the 50 where metrics differ most
Skip automatic metrics and rely entirely on human evaluation
Use LLM judge on a random 100-sample subset for both versions
questions.explanation: Stratified sampling maximises signal per dollar: cheap metrics quickly identify the most contentious cases where automated lexical scores disagree. Applying expensive LLM judge only to these high-signal samples gives the most informative quality signal for the lowest cost.
